verses.text: The child Samuel ministered unto the LORD before Eli. And the word of the LORD was precious in those days; there was no open vision. It came to pass at that time, when Eli was laid down in his place, and his eyes began to wax dim, that he could not see; and ere the lamp of God went out in the temple of the LORD, where the ark of God was, and Samuel was laid down to sleep. Guidance: 'The word of the LORD was precious' — Hebrew yaqar, meaning 'rare, scarce.' Open visions had dried up. The era of the judges had silenced even prophecy. Eli's eyes 'wax dim' physically; Israel's eyes have dimmed spiritually. And yet the 'lamp of God' is still burning — the menorah in the tabernacle had not yet been extinguished for the night. The setting is precisely chosen: in the literal last flicker before dawn, the LORD speaks. He often speaks just before the light is supposed to go out, never after.

That the LORD called Samuel: and he answered, Here am I. He ran unto Eli, and said, Here am I; for thou calledst me. And he said, I called not; lie down again. And he went and lay down. And the LORD called yet again, Samuel. And Samuel arose and went to Eli, and said, Here am I; for thou didst call me. And he answered, I called not, my son; lie down again. Now Samuel did not yet know the LORD, neither was the word of the LORD yet revealed unto him. Guidance: Twice the boy hears his name and runs to the wrong room. 'Here am I' (Heb hinneni) — the response of every called servant in scripture from Abraham to Isaiah. He uses the right word; he just has the wrong address. 'Samuel did not yet know the LORD' is not a moral failure but a developmental one — he had been ministering before God all his short life, but he had not yet personally heard God's voice. Many faithful children of the covenant know about the LORD before they know the LORD. Conversion is the day the address book finally has the right name in it.

The LORD called Samuel again the third time. And he arose and went to Eli, and said, Here am I; for thou didst call me. And Eli perceived that the LORD had called the child. Therefore Eli said unto Samuel, Go, lie down: and it shall be, if he call thee, that thou shalt say, Speak, LORD; for thy servant heareth. So Samuel went and lay down in his place. Guidance: On the third visit Eli figures it out. To his credit, the old priest — disqualified, ineffective in his own family, blamed by the prophet last chapter — still teaches the boy the right reply: 'Speak, LORD; for thy servant heareth.' Even compromised leaders can pass on accurate instruction. Eli's last useful pastoral moment is helping a child hear God for himself. The mentor's gift is sometimes simply naming what is happening. Samuel goes back to bed instructed; the next call will land in a heart ready to listen.

The LORD came, and stood, and called as at other times, Samuel, Samuel. Then Samuel answered, Speak; for thy servant heareth. Guidance: 'The LORD came, and stood, and called' — the language is physical, theophanic. God is no longer disembodied speech; He is present and addressed. The double 'Samuel, Samuel' is the LORD's pattern with people He is calling intimately — 'Abraham, Abraham' (Gen 22), 'Moses, Moses' (Exod 3), 'Saul, Saul' (Acts 9). Samuel slightly trims the script he was given — he leaves out 'LORD' (some early manuscripts include it; the Hebrew Masoretic does not). Either way, the heart is right: 'Speak, for thy servant heareth.' The boy who could not yet pray has just spoken the simplest, deepest prayer in the book.

The LORD said to Samuel, Behold, I will do a thing in Israel, at which both the ears of every one that heareth it shall tingle. In that day I will perform against Eli all things which I have spoken concerning his house: when I begin, I will also make an end. For I have told him that I will judge his house for ever for the iniquity which he knoweth; because his sons made themselves vile, and he restrained them not. Therefore I have sworn unto the house of Eli, that the iniquity of Eli's house shall not be purged with sacrifice nor offering for ever. Guidance: God's first words to Samuel are a verdict on the man sleeping in the next room. 'Both ears tingle' is a phrase reserved for the most stunning judgments (used again of Jerusalem's fall in 2 Kings 21:12). The LORD repeats and seals the prophecy of chapter 2: Eli's house is finished. The verb 'restrained them not' is the chapter's verdict on the father — he had heard, he had warned, he had not restrained. Knowing the iniquity and not stopping it is itself a confirmation of it. The judgment 'shall not be purged with sacrifice' — there is no offering Eli can make to undo it. Some sins reach the point of finality where the system of sacrifices cannot reverse them; only judgment is left.

Samuel lay until the morning, and opened the doors of the house of the LORD. And Samuel feared to shew Eli the vision. Then Eli called Samuel, and said, Samuel, my son. And he answered, Here am I. And he said, What is the thing that the LORD hath said unto thee? I pray thee hide it not from me: God do so to thee, and more also, if thou hide any thing from me of all the things that he said unto thee. And Samuel told him every whit, and hid nothing from him. And he said, It is the LORD: let him do what seemeth him good. Guidance: Samuel does his usual morning chores — opens the temple doors — while carrying a word that will end the household he serves. He is afraid. Twelve-year-olds were not expected to deliver judgment oracles to high priests. But Eli demands the full truth with an oath ('God do so to thee, and more also, if thou hide any thing'). Samuel tells him 'every whit.' Eli's response is striking: 'It is the LORD: let him do what seemeth him good.' He neither defends himself nor argues; he accepts the verdict. The same passivity that doomed his sons now lets him receive the judgment without protest. The chapter does not say he repented — he could have — but he at least did not deny.

Samuel grew, and the LORD was with him, and did let none of his words fall to the ground. All Israel from Dan even to Beer-sheba knew that Samuel was established to be a prophet of the LORD. And the LORD appeared again in Shiloh: for the LORD revealed himself to Samuel in Shiloh by the word of the LORD. Guidance: Three statements about Samuel's maturity. 'The LORD was with him' — the highest possible verdict on a man's life (cf. Gen 39:2 of Joseph). 'None of his words fall to the ground' — every prophecy verified. 'Established to be a prophet' — a title that has been silent in Israel for generations is now reassigned to a single name. The book ends the chapter with a turning point: 'the LORD appeared again in Shiloh' — the period of yaqar (precious, rare revelation) is over. Samuel will hear from God again and again. The conversation that started in a small voice at midnight has become a national resource. A nation's silence often ends with one child saying 'Speak, for thy servant heareth.'
